subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
question: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
solution:
The position vector of center of mass r→cm is given as 

r→cm=Xcmx^+Ycm y^

Where, Xcm=x-coordinate of center of mass  and Ycm=y-coordinate of center of mass

r→cm=m1x1+m2x2+m3x3m1+m2+m3 x^ +m1y1+m2y2+m3y3m1+m2+m3 y^

r→cm=2m×L+m×2L+m5L22m+m+mx^+2m×L+m×L2+m×02m+m+my^

r→cm=13L8x^+5L8y^

q: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
question: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
solution:
From momentum conservation, Pi0=Pf
mu=Mv …i
From angular momentum conservation about O,
mu·L2=ML212ω
⇒ω=6muML …(ii)
From e=R.V·SR.V·A
1=V+ωL2u

V+ωL2=u
V+3muM=u
muM+3muM=u
4muM=u
mM=14
x=4

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: An average force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is applied on a machine gun firing bullets each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> at the speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> to keep it in position. The number of bullets fired per second by the machine gun is :
question: An average force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is applied on a machine gun firing bullets each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> at the speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> to keep it in position. The number of bullets fired per second by the machine gun is :
solution:
The formula to calculate the net momentum of the gun and bullet system can be written as

F=nmv   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required number of bullets.

125=n×101000×(250)⇒n=50

q: A rubber ball is released from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the floor. It bounces back repeatedly, always rising to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>81</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math> of the height through which it falls. Find the average speed of the ball. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A rubber ball is released from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the floor. It bounces back repeatedly, always rising to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>81</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math> of the height through which it falls. Find the average speed of the ball. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
v0=2gh

v=e2gh=2gh

⇒e=0.9

S=h+2e2h+2e4h+………

t=2h g+2e2h g+2e22h g+………

vav=st=2.5 m s-1

q: A body <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. It collides elastically with another body <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of the same mass which has an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. After the collision, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after the collision is written as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: A body <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. It collides elastically with another body <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of the same mass which has an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. After the collision, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after the collision is written as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Using the principle of conservation of linear momentum

0.1×3i^ + 0.1×5j^ = 0.1×4i^+j^ + 0.1×v→

v→=-i^ + j^ m s-1

v=v→=2 m s-1

KB=12×0.1×v2=110 J

x=1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> collides head on elastically with a stationary body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. After collision, the smaller body reverses its direction of motion and moves with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The initial speed of the smaller body before collision is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> collides head on elastically with a stationary body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. After collision, the smaller body reverses its direction of motion and moves with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The initial speed of the smaller body before collision is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Let u1 be the initial speed of the smaller body before collision. 

Let v be the final speed of the bigger body after collision.

Applying conservation of linear momentum,

1×u1+0=-2+3v⇒u1=-2+3v    ...1 

We know, coefficient of restitution for elastic perfectly collision is 1.

 ⇒1=v+2u1⇒v+2=u1    ...2 

Equating 1 and 2, we have

u1=4 m s-1.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ falls freely from a height of $100 \mathrm{~m}$, on a platform of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ which is mounted on a spring having spring constant $\mathrm{k}=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The bodysticks to the platform and the spring's maximum compression is found to be $x$. Given that $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2},$ the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be close to :
question: A body of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ falls freely from a height of $100 \mathrm{~m}$, on a platform of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ which is mounted on a spring having spring constant $\mathrm{k}=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The bodysticks to the platform and the spring's maximum compression is found to be $x$. Given that $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2},$ the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be close to :
solution: Velocity of $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ block just before it collides with $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ block $=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}}=\sqrt{2000} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Using principle of conservation of linear momentum just before and just after collision, we get $1 \times \sqrt{2000}=4 \mathrm{v} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{\sqrt{2000}}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Initial compression of spring $1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{x}_{0}=30 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_{0} \approx 0$ using work energy theorem, $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\Delta \mathrm{KE}$ $\Rightarrow 40 \times x+\frac{1}{2} \times 1.25 \times 10^{6}\left(0^{2}-x^{2}\right)$ $=0-\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times v^{2}$ solving $x \approx 4 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and Length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is applied to end B. The time taken by the rod to turn through at right angles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ____.
question: A uniform rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and Length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is applied to end B. The time taken by the rod to turn through at right angles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ____.
solution:
Impulse J=0.2 N s

⇒J=∫Fdt=0.2 N s

Now, angular impulse (M→) will be

Mc=∫τdt

=∫FL2dt

=L2∫Fdt=L2×J

=0.32×0.2

=0.03

Moment of inertia of the rod about the centre of mass, Icm=ML212=2×(0.3)212=0.096

Angular impulse will be equal to the change in angular momentum.

M=Icmωf-ωi

⇒0.03=0.096ωf

⇒ωf=2 rad s-1

For angular displacement, we can write θ=ωt

⇒t=θω=π2×2=π4 s.

Therefore, x=4.

q: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts, in such a way that ratio of their nuclear sizes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Their respective speed have a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is_________
question: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts, in such a way that ratio of their nuclear sizes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Their respective speed have a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is_________
solution:
Using the law of Momentum conservation

m1v1+m2(-v2)=0
⇒m1v1=m2v2
⇒m1m2=v2v1⇒4πρr3134πρr323=v2v1

It is given

 r1r2=1213
and r1r23=v2v1Therefore,12=v2v1
⇒v1v2=21

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A mass ' $m$ ' moves with a velocity $v$ and collides inelastically with another identical mass. After collision the $1^{\text {st }}$ mass moves with velocity $v / \sqrt{3}$ in a direction perpendicular to the initial direction of motion. Find the speed of the $2^{\text {nd }}$ mass after collision
question: A mass ' $m$ ' moves with a velocity $v$ and collides inelastically with another identical mass. After collision the $1^{\text {st }}$ mass moves with velocity $v / \sqrt{3}$ in a direction perpendicular to the initial direction of motion. Find the speed of the $2^{\text {nd }}$ mass after collision
solution: $m v=m v_1 \cos \theta$ $0=\frac{m v}{\sqrt{3}}-m v_1 \sin \theta$ $\therefore v_1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} v$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two identical particles move towards each other with velocity $2 v$ and $v$ respectively. The velocity of centre of mass is
question: Two identical particles move towards each other with velocity $2 v$ and $v$ respectively. The velocity of centre of mass is
solution:
Conserving Linear Momentum
$$
2 \mathrm{Mv}_{\mathrm{c}}=2 \mathrm{Mv}-\mathrm{Mv} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{v} / 2
$$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A man of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is running on the road and suddenly jumps into a stationary trolly car of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. Then the trolly car starts moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of the running man was _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, when he jumps into the car.
question: A man of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is running on the road and suddenly jumps into a stationary trolly car of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. Then the trolly car starts moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of the running man was _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, when he jumps into the car.
solution:
Applying conservation of the momentum,

mmu=mm+mtv⇒60u=60+120×2⇒u=6 m s-1

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The mass of a hydrogen molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.32</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27 </mn></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math> hydrogen molecules strike, per second, a fixed wall of the area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> to the normal, and rebound elastically with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the pressure on the wall is nearly:
question: The mass of a hydrogen molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.32</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27 </mn></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math> hydrogen molecules strike, per second, a fixed wall of the area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> to the normal, and rebound elastically with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the pressure on the wall is nearly:
solution:
F=2mnVcosθ⇒P=2mnVcosθA

⇒P=2×3.32×10-27×1023×1032×2×10-4

⇒P=2.35×103 N m-2

question: A uniform rectangular thin sheet ABCD of mass M has length a and breadth b, as shown in the figure. If the shaded portion HBGO is cut-off, the coordinates of the centre of mass of the remaining portion will be:
solution:
If the total mass of object mass M then the mass removed part will be M4 .

XCM=M·a2-M43a4M-M4=Ma2-3Ma163M4=512a
YCM=M·b2-M43b4M-M4=Mb2-3Mb163M4=512b

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A spherical body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is dropped from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the ground. After hitting the ground, the body rebounds to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The impulse of force imparted by the ground to the body is given by: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A spherical body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is dropped from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the ground. After hitting the ground, the body rebounds to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The impulse of force imparted by the ground to the body is given by: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Using equation of motion with constant acceleration, we can write:

v1=2gh1 and v2=2gh2

Now, required impulse

I→=Δp→=p→f-p→i

Given, M=0.1 kg

Therefore,

I=Δp=0.1(2×9.8×5-(-2×9.8×10))

=0.1(72+14)≈2.39 kg m s-1

q:
This question has Statement - I and Statement - II  of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement - I: A point particle of mass m moving with speed ν collides with stationary point particle of mass M. If the maximum energy loss possible is given as
f12mν2 then f=mM+m.

Statement - II: Maximum energy loss occurs when the particles get stuck together as a result of the collision.
question:
This question has Statement - I and Statement - II  of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement - I: A point particle of mass m moving with speed ν collides with stationary point particle of mass M. If the maximum energy loss possible is given as
f12mν2 then f=mM+m.

Statement - II: Maximum energy loss occurs when the particles get stuck together as a result of the collision.
options: Statement-<math><mrow><mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math><mrow><mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is false.
Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is false, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true.

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is a correct explanation of Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math>.

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is not a correct explanation of Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math>.
solution:
Maximum energy loss will take place in perfectly inelastic collision, and is given by, ∆K=P22m-P22m+M
⇒∆K=P22m1-mm+M
⇒∆K=P22mMm+M
⇒∆K=12mv2Mm+M
On comparing with the given form of equation, we have, f=Mm+M

Hence Statement- I is false, Statement- II is true. ( As it is perfect Inelastic collision)

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
Statement-1 : Two particles moving in the same direction do not lose all their energy in a completely inelastic collision.
Statement-2 : Principle of conservation of momentum holds true for all kinds of collisions.
Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
question:
Statement-1 : Two particles moving in the same direction do not lose all their energy in a completely inelastic collision.
Statement-2 : Principle of conservation of momentum holds true for all kinds of collisions.
Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement1
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.
solution: If it is a completely inelastic collision then $$ \begin{gathered} m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2=m_1 v+m_2 v \\ v=\frac{m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2}{m_1+m_2} \\ \mathrm{~K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{p}_1^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{p}_2^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}_2} \end{gathered} $$ as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}_1$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}_2}$ both simultaneously cannot be zero therefore total KE cannot be lost.

subject: Physics

q: A player caught a cricket ball of mass $150 \mathrm{~g}$ moving at a speed of $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If the catching process is completed in $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$, the magnitude of force exerted by the ball on the hand of the player is:
question: A player caught a cricket ball of mass $150 \mathrm{~g}$ moving at a speed of $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If the catching process is completed in $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$, the magnitude of force exerted by the ball on the hand of the player is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}-0}{0.1} \\ & =\frac{150 \times 10^{-3} \times 20}{0.1}=30 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with a hydrogen atom at rest. Soon after the collision, the particle comes to rest, and the atom recoils and goes to its first excited state. The initial kinetic energy of the particle (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>eV</mi></math>) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>N</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is: (Given the mass of the hydrogen atom to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GeV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>).........
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with a hydrogen atom at rest. Soon after the collision, the particle comes to rest, and the atom recoils and goes to its first excited state. The initial kinetic energy of the particle (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>eV</mi></math>) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>N</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is: (Given the mass of the hydrogen atom to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GeV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>).........
solution:
Given, M=200 MeV c-2 and m=1 GeV c-2, now use the concept of conservation of linear momentum, Mv0=mv⇒v=Mv0m  ...1.

Now use the conservation of energy, 

12Mv02=12mv2+∆E  ..2, here ∆E=13.6 eV, for the hydrogen atom in the ground state, now from equation, 1 & 2, 12Mv02=143∆E×108 eV=N4,

⇒N4=514 eV⇒N=51.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: When kinetic energy of a body becomes 36 times of its original value, the percentage increase in the momentum of the body will be:
question: When kinetic energy of a body becomes 36 times of its original value, the percentage increase in the momentum of the body will be:
solution:
Kinetic energy $(K)=\frac{P^2}{2 m}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}$
If $K_f=36 K_i$ So, $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}=6 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}$ $\begin{aligned} \% \text { increase in momentum } & =\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{6 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}} \times 100 \% \\ & =500 \% \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A projectile moving vertically upwards with a velocity of $200 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ breaks into two equal parts at a height of $490 \mathrm{~m}$. One part starts moving vertically upwards with a velocity of $400 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. How much time it will take, after the break up with the other part to hit the ground?
question: A projectile moving vertically upwards with a velocity of $200 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ breaks into two equal parts at a height of $490 \mathrm{~m}$. One part starts moving vertically upwards with a velocity of $400 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. How much time it will take, after the break up with the other part to hit the ground?
solution: Momentum before explosion = Momentum after explosion $$ \begin{gathered} m \times 200 \hat{j}=\frac{m}{2} \times 400 \hat{j}+\frac{m}{2} v \\ =\frac{m}{2}(400 \hat{j}+v) \\ \Rightarrow \quad 400 \hat{j}-400 \hat{j}=v \\ \therefore \quad v=0 \end{gathered} $$ i.e., the velocity of the other part of the mass, $v=0$ Let time taken to reach the earth by this part be $t$ Applying formula, $h=u t+\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ $$ \begin{aligned} & 490=0+\frac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times t^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t^2=\frac{980}{9.8}=100 \\ & \therefore \quad t=\sqrt{100}=10 \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

question: If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>22</mn></mrow></msup></math> gas molecules each of mass <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup></math> kg collides with a surface (perpendicular to it) elastically per second over an area <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> with a speed <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo></math> the pressure exerted by the gas molecules will be of the order of:
solution:
Total momentum transferred in one second =n2mv=10222×10-26×104=2
Force experienced by surface =2 N
Pressure on surface =ForceArea=21=2 Pa

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
What percentage of kinetic energy of a moving particle is transferred to a stationary particle when it strikes the stationary particle of 5 times its mass?
(Assume the collision to be head-on elastic collision)
question:
What percentage of kinetic energy of a moving particle is transferred to a stationary particle when it strikes the stationary particle of 5 times its mass?
(Assume the collision to be head-on elastic collision)
solution:
After collision,

Applying momentum conservation,

mu=mv1+5mv2  ⇒u=v1+5v2   ...1 

Coefficient of restitution, e=1=v2-v1u

∴ u=v2-v1   ...2

Adding  2u=6v2  ⇒v2=u3 and  u=u3-v1

∴ v1=u3-u=-2u3

Percentage change in kinetic energy =12mv12-12mu212mu2×100

=v12-u2u2×100=4u29-u2u2×100  =-59×100  =55.6%

q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and another of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are moving with equal kinetic energy. The ratio of their respective momenta will be
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and another of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are moving with equal kinetic energy. The ratio of their respective momenta will be
solution:
Given: The mass of the objects m1=8 kg and  m2=2 kg.

Let the momentum of the 8 kg and 2 kg masses is p1 and p2, respectively, then according to question, their kinetic energy are equal, i.e.,

K1=K2

p122m1=p222m2

p1p2=m1m2=41=21

At the same time another particle of the same mass is thrown vertically upwards from the ground with a speed of <math><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>h</mi></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> If they collide head-on completely inelastically, the time taken for the combined mass to reach the ground, in units of <math><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math> is:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is dropped from a height <math><mi>h</mi></math> above the ground. At the same time another particle of the same mass is thrown vertically upwards from the ground with a speed of <math><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>h</mi></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> If they collide head-on completely inelastically, the time taken for the combined mass to reach the ground, in units of <math><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math> is:
solution:
time for collision t1=h2gh
After t1
VA=0-gt1=-gh2
and VB=2gh-gt1=gh2-12
at the time of collision
p→i=pf→
⇒mV→A+mV→B=2mV→f
⇒-gh2+gh2-12=2V→f
Vf=0

and height from ground

=h-12gt12=h-h4=3h4

so time ,

t=2×3h4g=32 hg

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: For a mechanical system of many particles total kinetic energy is the sum of kinetic energies of all the particles.
Statement II: The total kinetic energy can be the sum of kinetic energy of the center of mass w.r.t to the origin and the kinetic energy of all the particles w.r.t. the center of mass as the reference.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: For a mechanical system of many particles total kinetic energy is the sum of kinetic energies of all the particles.
Statement II: The total kinetic energy can be the sum of kinetic energy of the center of mass w.r.t to the origin and the kinetic energy of all the particles w.r.t. the center of mass as the reference.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
Statement I: Total kinetic energy of a system equals $\sum_i \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i^2$. This is TRUE by definition.
Statement II: Total KE can be decomposed as $KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2}M v_{cm}^2 + \sum_i \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i'^2$, where the first term is kinetic energy of center of mass motion and the second is kinetic energy of particles relative to the center of mass. This is TRUE by the energy decomposition theorem (Koenig's theorem).
Both statements are true.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two identical bodies $A$ and $B$ of equal masses have initial velocities $\vec{v_1} = 4\hat{i}$ m/s and $\vec{v_2} = 4\hat{j}$ m/s respectively. The body $A$ has acceleration $\vec{a_1} = 6\hat{i} + 6\hat{j}$ m/s$^2$ while the acceleration of the other body $B$ is zero. The centre of mass of the two bodies moves in __________ path.
question: Two identical bodies $A$ and $B$ of equal masses have initial velocities $\vec{v_1} = 4\hat{i}$ m/s and $\vec{v_2} = 4\hat{j}$ m/s respectively. The body $A$ has acceleration $\vec{a_1} = 6\hat{i} + 6\hat{j}$ m/s$^2$ while the acceleration of the other body $B$ is zero. The centre of mass of the two bodies moves in __________ path.
solution:
Velocity of the centre of mass is given by:

$\vec{v}_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1\vec{v}_1 + m_2\vec{v}_2}{m_1 + m_2}$

Since $m_1 = m_2 = m$, we get:

$\vec{v}_{cm} = \dfrac{m(4\hat{i}) + m(4\hat{j})}{2m} = 2\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}$ m/s

Acceleration of the centre of mass is given by:

$\vec{a}_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1\vec{a}_1 + m_2\vec{a}_2}{m_1 + m_2}$

$\vec{a}_{cm} = \dfrac{m(6\hat{i} + 6\hat{j}) + 0}{2m} = 3\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}$ m/s$^2$

Here, $\vec{v}_{cm} = 2(\hat{i} + \hat{j})$ and $\vec{a}_{cm} = 3(\hat{i} + \hat{j})$.

Since the initial velocity and acceleration of the centre of mass are parallel to each other, the centre of mass moves in a straight line path.

Answer: straight line

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: As shown below, bob \(A\) of a pendulum having massless string of length ' \(R\) ' is released from \(60^{\circ}\) to the vertical. It hits another bob \(B\) of half the mass that is at rest on a friction less table in the center. Assuming elastic collision, the magnitude of the velocity of bob A after the collision will be (take g as acceleration due to gravity.)
question: As shown below, bob \(A\) of a pendulum having massless string of length ' \(R\) ' is released from \(60^{\circ}\) to the vertical. It hits another bob \(B\) of half the mass that is at rest on a friction less table in the center. Assuming elastic collision, the magnitude of the velocity of bob A after the collision will be (take g as acceleration due to gravity.)
solution: Velocity of a just before hitting : $\mathrm{u}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~g} \frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{gR}}$ Just after collision, let velocity of A and B are $\mathrm{v}_1$ and $v_2$ respectively $\therefore \text { by COM: }$ $\begin{aligned} & m u=m v_1+\frac{m}{2} v_2 \\ & 2 v_1+v_2=2 u ...(i)\\ & e=1=\frac{v_2-v_1}{u} \\ & \Rightarrow v_2-v_1=u..(ii) \end{aligned}$ From (i) -(ii) $\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{v}_1=\mathrm{u} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_1=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{3}=\frac{1}{3} \sqrt{\mathrm{gR}}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A batsman hits back a ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> straight in the direction of the bowler without changing its initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The impulse imparted to the ball is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>.
question: A batsman hits back a ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> straight in the direction of the bowler without changing its initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The impulse imparted to the ball is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>.
solution:
Impulse, J=∫Fdt=Change in momentum

Therefore,
Impulse, Δp=mv--mv=2mv=2×0.4×15 =12 N s

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A ball with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another identical ball at rest. After the collision, the direction of each ball makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the original direction. If the ratio of velocities of the balls after the collision is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>y</mi></math>, then what is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>?
question: A ball with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another identical ball at rest. After the collision, the direction of each ball makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the original direction. If the ratio of velocities of the balls after the collision is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>y</mi></math>, then what is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>?
solution:
From conservation of momentum along y-axis.

P→iy=P→fy

0+0=mv1sin30°j^+mv2sin30°-j^

mv2sin30°=mv1sin30°

v2=v1 or v1v2=1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
Figures (a), (b), (c) and (d) show variation of force with time.

The impulse is highest in figure.
question:
Figures (a), (b), (c) and (d) show variation of force with time.

The impulse is highest in figure.
solution:
As we know, impulse is the area under the curve of the force vs. time graph. So, analysing each figure area under curve.

a A1=12×1.0×0.5=14 N‐s

b A2=0.5×2=1 N‐s

c A3=12×1×0.75=38 N‐s

d A4=12×2×0.5=12 N‐s

From above calculations, area under curve in figure (b) is maximum. Thus, fig (b) impulse is highest.
